summary:
The Folsom tradition was a Paleo-Indian culture of the North American Great Plains, dated to approximately 10,800–10,000 BCE, that succeeded the [[event:1283|Clovis culture]] and adapted to the dramatically changed ecological conditions following the [[event:1289|extinction of Pleistocene megafauna]]. Folsom hunters specialized in hunting Bison antiquus, a now-extinct large bison species, using distinctive thin, finely fluted spear points of exceptional craftsmanship known as Folsom points.

The type site at Folsom, New Mexico, was discovered in 1927 when a Black cowboy named George McJunkin found fossilized bison bones and a distinctive stone point in a dried-up arroyo — a discovery that proved for the first time that humans had been in North America during the Pleistocene, coexisting with now-extinct animal species. Folsom communities were highly mobile hunters, following bison herds across the grasslands of the Great Plains. Their campsites are typically small and ephemeral, reflecting a nomadic lifestyle organized around seasonal bison hunting, sometimes involving coordinated communal hunts that drove animals into gullies or over cliffs. The Folsom tradition represents the successful adaptation of early Americans to a post-megafauna world dominated by bison.
